title: Gluten-free chocolate cake
subtitle: Rich, moist chocolate cake made with almond and rice flour
excerpt: A deeply chocolatey layer cake made without wheat flour: almond flour provides fat and tenderness, rice flour gives body, and dark chocolate melted into the batter gives an intensity that a cocoa-only cake cannot match. The crumb is slightly denser than a standard Victoria sponge but stays moist for days. Filled and topped with a dark chocolate ganache.
sourceNotes: Flourless and low-flour chocolate cakes using ground almonds or nut flours are documented in European baking literature from the late nineteenth century, particularly in the Austro-Hungarian tradition. Antoine Carême, Le Pâtissier Royal Parisien (1815, public domain), describes rich chocolate preparations using ground almonds. Recipes using nut-based flours as a wheat substitute in chocolate cakes appear in British and European domestic cookery books published before 1928.

recipe.freezeNotes: Freeze the sponge layers unfilled, wrapped tightly. Defrost at room temperature and fill with freshly made ganache.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: The baked, unfilled sponge layers keep well wrapped at room temperature for 2 days or refrigerated for 4 days. The ganache-filled cake keeps refrigerated for 3 days; bring to room temperature for 30 minutes before serving.

baking.bakeTemperatureNote: conventional oven. Fan at 160°C. Bake for 30 to 35 minutes; gluten-free cakes can appear set on top before the interior is done, so use a skewer.

recipeTools.notes: Two tins if you have them; otherwise bake the layers in sequence.

glossaryTerms.definition: Gluten-free cakes set differently from wheat-flour cakes: the surface can look and feel set while the centre is still under-baked and will sink on cooling. Always test with a skewer inserted into the very centre of the cake, checking it comes out with no wet batter clinging to it. Slight moisture from the chocolate is normal; raw batter is not. Do not test before the minimum bake time as opening the oven early causes the centre to collapse.

content.text: The key difference with gluten-free baking is that there is no gluten network to hold the cake together as it cools, so the structure relies entirely on the eggs setting. Do not underbake:
test with a skewer before removing from the oven
and cool completely in the tin before turning out. A warm gluten-free cake is fragile and will tear.

Heat the oven to 180°C conventional (160°C fan). Grease and line two 20 cm round tins. Melt the chocolate and butter together in a heatproof bowl set over barely simmering water, stirring occasionally. Remove from the heat and stir in the sugar. Beat in the eggs one at a time. Fold in the almond flour, rice flour, cocoa powder, baking powder, and vanilla extract until just combined.

Divide the batter evenly between the tins and smooth the surfaces. Bake for 30 to 35 minutes until a skewer inserted in the centre comes out with no wet batter. Cool completely in the tins before turning out.

Heat the cream until just simmering. Pour over the chopped chocolate in a heatproof bowl and leave for 2 minutes, then stir until smooth. Allow to cool at room temperature until thick enough to spread (about 30 minutes, or 15 minutes in the refrigerator). Spread half the ganache over one cooled sponge layer, place the second layer on top, and spread the remaining ganache over the top and sides.
